source_metadata.title_raw: Molly S. Stump - A-18-106 - May 30, 2018 - Palo Alto

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 30, 2018

Molly S. Stump
Palo Alto City Attorney
250 Hamilton Avenue, 8th Floor
Palo Alto, CA 94301

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-106

Dear Ms. Stump:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Palo Alto Mayor Liz Kniss, Vice Mayor Eric Filseth, and City Councilmember Tom DuBois regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

Does the Act prohibit:

(1) Mayor Kniss from taking part in governmental decisions relating to at-grade crossings of the Caltrain rail corridor tracks that bisect the City given that Mayor Kniss have an ownership interest in a multi-family residential rental property located within 500 feet of the tracks?

(2) Vice Mayor Filseth from taking part in those decisions given that he has an ownership interest in his single-family residence, located within 500 feet of the tracks and the Palo Alto Avenue at-grade crossing?

(3) Councilmember DuBois from taking part in those decisions given that he leases his residence located within 500 feet of the tracks and the Meadow Drive at-grade crossing?

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-106
Page No. 2

CONCLUSIONS

(1) Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the at-grade crossings
because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her
business interest in her multi-family residential real property.

(2) Yes. The Act prohibits the Vice Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would
have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on his residence under Regulation
18702.2(a)(11).

(3) No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because
they would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his leasehold interest
in his residence under Regulation 18702.2(b).

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR

The Caltrain rail corridor bisects the City of Palo Alto, separating the northeastern portion of
the City from the southwestern portion. The rail corridor runs parallel to Alma Street and passes
close by many residential parcels within the City. The State's High Speed Rail Authority and
Caltrain intend to increase both the frequency and length of trains running on these tracks. Toward
that end, the Caltrain Joint Powers Board has begun a project to transition the Caltrain system from
diesel to electric power. This project will change the infrastructure along the Caltrain system's
right-of-way by adding aerial wires ("catenary wires"), thereby enabling the use of trains that are
quieter, faster, and more frequent. Caltrain expects to begin electrified service in 2022.

Responding in part to the prospect of more frequent trains, San Francisco Peninsula cities
with east-west streets that cross the tracks at grade level, including the City of Palo Alto, have
begun planning to separate those streets from the grade of the tracks to limit interruption of traffic
circulation and to increase safety. The City currently has three separated roadway crossings and
four at-grade roadway crossings of the tracks.

The City has conducted vehicle circulation studies that have shown that increased gate-
down time at the at-grade crossings resulting from the increased use of the tracks will be felt
throughout the City. However, it has not yet determined which crossings will be modified, how they
will be modified, or in what order they will be modified. Commencement of the City's grade-
crossing modification projects will not begin until 2023 at the earliest, and completion of the
projects will take a decade or more.

The City is currently engaged in preliminary planning for grade separating the four City
streets that cross the tracks: Palo Alto Avenue, Churchill Avenue, Meadow Drive, and Charleston
Road. There are numerous options for separating the train grade from that of cars, bicycles, and
pedestrians at the City's at-grade crossings. A non-exhaustive list of potential options includes:
raising the tracks above the street or depressing the street under the tracks; raising the street over the
tracks; or closing each at-grade crossing. Depending on the configuration of the grade separation at
issue and the option or options selected, a grade separation could require public acquisition of
nearby private property.

File No. A-18-106
Page No. 3

In addition to considering projects at the four at-grade crossings, the planning process has
also included consideration of "citywide options" in which the entire Caltrain right-of-way would
be moved below ground level, into a trench or underground tunnel. Either of these citywide options
would result in impacts along the entire length of the tracks, including necessary changes to the
existing at-grade and grade-separated crossings and to the City's two existing train stations at
University Avenue and California Avenue.

All potential projects will involve at least some limited-term construction impacts. For some
options, construction impacts would be substantial and last two to five years or longer. In addition, because trains must continue to run while projects are constructed,
options that require the tracks to be moved will also require significant temporary replacement
tracks to be built to enable ongoing operation of the corridor during construction.

The Palo Alto City Council has nine members including the Mayor and Vice Mayor. Mayor
Kniss has an ownership interest in a multi-family residential rental property in the City in which she
does not reside. That property is within 500 feet of the Caltrain tracks, but is not nearby any of the
City's four at-grade crossings. Vice Mayor Filseth has an ownership interest in his single-family
residence, which serves as his primary residence, located within 500 feet of the tracks and the
existing at-grade crossing at Palo Alto Avenue.

Councilmember DuBois has a leasehold interest in his residence, a rented townhouse,
located within 500 feet of the tracks and the at-grade crossing at Meadow Drive. Until recently, the
Councilmember was a long-time home owner in the City. Three years ago, the Councilmember and
his then-wife began marital dissolution proceedings and sold their home. The Councilmember
subsequently rented a residence for a two-year period that ended on April 8, 2018, and he recently
moved into the townhouse that serves as his current residence. The townhouse is convenient for his
daughter, who has two years of high school remaining.

You state that Councilmember DuBois intends to purchase a home soon, and is actively
monitoring real estate listings and attending open houses. You also state that his lease can be broken
at any time, and that if he breaks the lease, he would only be responsible for rent until the
townhouse is re-rented. You also note that finding a new tenant for rental property within the City
does not typically take long in the City's current rental market.

ANALYSIS

The Act prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision if that
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's
financial interests distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and
87103.) The Act identifies an official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of
interest in Section 87103. Of those interests, the officials at issue each has a real property interest in
his or her real property at issue, assuming each has an interest of $2,000 or more in that real
property, and an interest in his or her personal finances or those of immediate family members, with
respect to the decisions at issue.

File No. A-18-106
Page No. 4

In addition, the Mayor also has: a business interest in her property management business
that manages the rental of her multi-family residential rental property if she has an investment
worth $2,000 or more in that business; a source of income interest in that business if she receives
$500 or more from it in the 12 months preceding the decision at issue; and a source of income
interest in any tenant of that business from whom she receives $500 or more over that time period.

Foreseeability & Materiality

Whether a decision's effect on an official's financial interest is reasonably foreseeable is
determined pursuant to Regulation 18701. Under subdivision (a) of that regulation, a decision's
effect is presumed reasonably foreseeable when the official's financial interest is "explicitly
involved" in the decision, and an official's financial interest is explicitly involved if the decision
"involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other
entitlement to, or contract with, the financial interest." None of the Mayor's, Vice Mayor's, or
Councilmember's respective financial interests is explicitly involved in the decisions relating to the
City's at-grade crossings.

Regulation 18701(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's interest that is not
explicitly involved in the decision at issue is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

Mayor Kniss' Multi-Family Residential Rental Business

Regulation 18702.1(b) provides that a decision's effect on an official's business interest is
material "if a prudent person with sufficient information would find it is reasonably foreseeable that
the decision's financial effect would contribute to a change in … the value of a privately-held
business entity."

Mayor Kniss has a business interest in her multi-family residential rental property located
within 500 feet of the tracks. The decisions relating to the at-grade crossings include consideration
of the "citywide options" of placing the entire Caltrain right-of-way into a trench or underground
tunnel. The construction impacts from these citywide options would be substantial and would last
two to five years or longer. Because trains must continue to run while the potential projects are
constructed, options that require tracks to be moved, such as those contemplating placing the tracks
below ground, may require significant temporary replacement tracks to be built. Although demand
for residential rentals within the City is high, the Mayor's multi-family residential rental property is
in competition with comparable rental properties that are not located nearby potential long-term,
substantial construction. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that the effect of the decisions at
issue will contribute to a change in the value of the Mayor's business interest in her multi-family
residential rental property.

Vice Mayor Filseth's Single-Family Residence

Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(11), a decision's effect on an official's real property interest,
other than an interest in commercial property containing a business, is material if the decision
affects real property within 500 feet of the official's property, unless there are sufficient facts to

File No. A-18-106
Page No. 5

indicate that the decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's
property.

Vice Mayor Filseth's residence is within 500 feet of the tracks and the Palo Alto Avenue at-
grade crossing. Any project to modify the Palo Alto Avenue at-grade crossing would have a
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the Vice Mayor's residence due externalities
associated with nearby construction, including traffic, noise, or air-quality impacts. Furthermore, as
noted above, the decisions relating to the at-grade crossings include consideration of citywide
options, which may result in long-term substantial construction and associated externalities within
500 feet of the Vice Mayor's residence. Therefore, the facts presented indicate that the decisions at
issue will have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the Vice Mayor's residence.

Councilmember DuBois Leased Single-Family Residence

Regulation 18702.2(b), which sets forth the Act's materiality standard applicable to a
decision's effect on an official's leasehold interest in real property, provides that a decision's
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's leasehold is material if the decision will:

(1) Change the termination date of the lease;
(2) Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property;
(3) Increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the official has a right to sublease
    the property;
(4) Change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property;
(5) Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property.

The real property underlying the Councilmember's leasehold is within 500 feet of the tracks
and the existing at-grade crossing at Meadow Drive. The decisions relating to the at-grade crossings
will not affect the termination date of the Councilmember's lease. Because potential projects
relating to the at-grade crossings will not commence until 2023 at the earliest, more than four and a
half years in the future, the decisions at issue will not affect the present rental value of the real
property underlying the lease. Furthermore, any effect of those decisions on the potential rental
value of the real property due to project construction impacts is irrelevant to the Councilmember;
given that the he intends to purchase a home soon, and is actively working toward that end, he is
highly unlikely to still have any interest in the real property underlying the lease by the time project
construction impacts begin to be realized, no sooner than 2023. The decisions at issue will not
change the Councilmember's actual or legally allowable use of the real property. And finally, those
decisions will not impact his use and enjoyment of the real property because project construction
will not commence until long after the Councilmember intends to end the lease. Therefore, those
decisions would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the
Councilmember's leasehold interest in his residence.

Thus, the Act prohibits Mayor Kniss and Vice Mayor Filseth, but not Councilmember
DuBois, from taking part in decisions relating to the at-grade crossings.²

² We do not further analyze the Mayor or Vice Mayor's other interests in the decisions at issue because we
have already determined that the Act prohibits each of them from taking part in those decisions. We do not further
analyze the Councilmember's interest in his personal finances with respect to those decisions because Regulation

File No. A-18-106
Page No. 6

Public Generally Exception

The "public generally exception," set forth in Regulation 18703, provides that a decision's
effect on an official's interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the
official establishes that the decision affects a significant segment of the public and the effect on the
official's interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment. Neither you, the
Mayor, or the Vice Mayor has established that the decisions relating to the at-grade crossings affect
a significant segment of the public or that the effect on either the Mayor or Vice Mayor's respective
interests is not unique. Accordingly, the public generally exception is not applicable to the decisions
relating to the at-grade crossings based on the facts presented.3

Segmentation

The final issue to be considered is whether the decisions relating to each of the various
options for the at-grade crossings may be segmented pursuant to Regulation 18706. In some cases,
large, complex decisions may be segmented into separate decisions so that even if an official has a
disqualifying interest in one component of the series of decisions, that official may still participate
in the other components in which he or she does not have a disqualifying interest. (Borger Advice
Letter, No. I-15-110; and Whitman Advice Letter, No. A-14-186.) However, where a decision in
which an official has a disqualifying interest is "inextricably interrelated" to the other decisions that
comprise the series of decisions, the official is disqualified from taking part in all of those
decisions. (Mattas Advice Letter, No. A-16-113.) Regulation 18706(b) provides that "decisions are
'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affirm, nullify,
or alter the result of another decision."

The decisions relating to various options for the at-grade crossings within the City are a
series of decisions aimed at modifying the City's infrastructure that interfaces with the Caltrain rail
corridor to accommodate the corridor's upcoming changes enabling the use of trains that are
quieter, faster, and more frequent. The decisions relating to the at-grade crossings include
consideration of "citywide options" that would move the entire Caltrain right-of-way, including the
tracks, below ground. Because these citywide options, if adopted, would "effectively determine,
affirm, nullify, or alter the result of" other decisions relating to the at-grade crossings within the
City, the decisions relating to the citywide options are inextricably interrelated to the other
decisions regarding the at-grade crossings. Thus, at this time, it does not appear that the decisions
relating to the City's at-grade crossings are capable of segmentation.

18702.5(c) provides that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.2 when the decision at issue affects the
official's real property interest.

3 Under Regulation 18703(b), a "significant segment of the public" is at least 25 percent of all businesses or
nonprofit entities within the jurisdiction (paragraph (1)); all real property, commercial real property, or residential real
property within the jurisdiction (paragraph (2)); or all individuals within the jurisdiction (paragraph (3)). You should
seek further advice regarding the application of the public generally exception if the decisions at issue will affect a
significant segment of the public.

File No. A-18-106
Page No. 7

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Brian G. Lau
Acting General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit: 
(1) Mayor Kniss from taking part in governmental decisions relating to at-grade crossings of the 
Cal train rail corridor tracks that bisect the City given that Mayor Kniss has an ownership 
interest in a multi-family residential rental property located within 500 feet of the tracks? 
(2) Vice Mayor Filseth from taking part in those decisions given that he has an ownership interest 
in his single-family residence, located within 500 feet of the tracks and the Palo Alto Avenue at-
grade crossing? 
(3) Councilmember DuBois from taking part in those decisions given that he leases his residence 
located within 500 feet of the tracks and the Meadow Drive at-grade crossing?
sections.conclusion:
(1) Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the at-grade crossings 
because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her 
business interest in her multi-family residential real property. 
(2) Yes. The Act prohibits the Vice Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would 
have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on his residence under Regulation 
18702.2(a)(l l). 
(3) No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because 
they would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his leasehold interest 
in his residence under Regulation l 8702.2(b ). 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
The Caltrain rail corridor bisects the City of Palo Alto, separating the northeastern portion of 
the City from the southwestern portion. The rail corridor runs parallel to Alma Street and passes 
close by many residential parcels within the City. The State's High Speed Rail Authority and 
Caltrain intend to increase both the frequency and length of trains running on these tracks. Toward 
that end, the Caltrain Joint Powers Board has begun a project to transition the Caltrain system from 
diesel to electric power. This project will change the infrastructure along the Caltrain system's

circulation and to increase safety. The City currently has three separated roadway crossings and

will be modified, or in what order they will be modified. Commencement of the City's grade-
crossing modification projects will not begin until 2023 at the earliest, and completion of the 
projects will take a decade or more. 
The City is currently engaged in preliminary planning for grade separating the four City 
streets that cross the tracks: Palo Alto A venue, Churchill A venue, Meadow Drive, and Charleston 
Road. There are numerous options for separating the train grade from that of cars, bicycles, and 
pedestrians at the City's at-grade crossings. A non-exhaustive list of potential options includes: 
raising the tracks above the street or depressing the street under the tracks; raising the street over the 
tracks; or closing each at-grade crossing. Depending on the configuration of the particular crossing

nearby private property.  
In addition to considering projects at the four at-grade crossings, the planning process has 
also included consideration of"citywide options" in which the entire Caltrain right-of-way would 
be tnoved below ground level, into a trench or underground tunnel. Either of these citywide options

existing at-grade and grade-separated crossings and to the City's two existing train stations at 
University Avenue and California Avenue. 
All potential projects will involve at least some limited-term construction impacts. For some 
options, such as the citywide options, construction impacts would be substantial and last two to five 
years or longer. In addition, because trains must continue to run while projects are constructed, 
options that require the tracks to be moved will also require significant temporary replacement

does not reside. That property is within 500 feet of the Caltrain tracks, but is not nearby any of the 
City's four at-grade crossings. Vice Mayor Filseth has an ownership interest in his single-family 
residence, which serves as his primary residence, located within 500 feet of the tracks and the 
existing at-grade crossing at Palo Alto Avenue. 
Councilmember DuBois has a leasehold interest in his residence, a rented townhouse, 
located within 500 feet of the tracks and the at-grade crossing at Meadow Drive. Until recently, the 
Councilmember was a long-time home owner in the City. Three years ago, the Councilmember and 
his then-wife began marital dissolution proceedings and sold their home. The Councilmember 
subsequently rented a residence for a two-year period that ended on April 8, 2018, and he recently 
moved into the townhouse that serves as his current residence. The townhouse is convenient for his 
daughter, who has two years of high school remaining.

. 
The Act prohibits a public official from taking part in a governmental decision if that 
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's 
financial interests distinguishable from its effect on the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 
87103.) The Act identifies an official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying conflict of 
interest in Section 87103. Of those interests, the officials at issue each has a real property interest in

respect to the decisions at issue.

that manages the rental of her multi-family residential rental property if she has an investment 
worth $2,000 or more in that business; a source of income interest in that business if she receives 
$500 or more from it in the 12 months preceding the ~ecision at issue; and a source of income 
interest in any tenant of that business from whom she receives $500 or more over that time period.

realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical."

business entity." 
Mayor Kniss has a business interest in her multi-family residential rental property located

residential rental property.

Under Regulation 18702.2( a)(l 1 ), a decision's effect on an official's real property interest, 
other than an interest in commercial property containing a business, is material if the decision 
affects real property within 500 feet of the official's property, unless there are sufficient facts to  
indicate that the decision will not have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's

Vice Mayor Filseth's residence is within 500 feet of the tracks and the Palo Alto Avenue at-
grade crossing. Any project to modify the Palo Alto Avenue at-grade crossing would have a 
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the Vice Mayor's residence due externalities 
associated with nearby construction, including traffic, noise, or air-quality impacts. Furthennore, as

decision's effect on an official's leasehold interest in real property, provides that a decision's 
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on an official's leasehold is material if the decision will: 
(1) Change the tennination date of the lease; 
(2) Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; 
(3) Increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the official has a right to sublease 
the property; 
(4) Change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property; 
(5) Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property.

and the existing at-grade crossing at Meadow Drive. The decisions relating to the at-grade crossings

Public Generally Exception  
The "public generally exception," set forth in Regulation 18703, provides that a decision's 
effect on an official's interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that the decision affects a significant segment of the public and the effect on the 
official's interest is not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment. Neither you, the

The final issue to be considered is whether the decisions relating to each of the various 
options for the at-grade crossings may be segmented pursuant to Regulation 18706. In some cases,

Letter, No. 1-15-110; and Whitman Advice Letter, No. A-14-186.) However, where a decision in 
which an official has a disqualifying interest is "inextricably interrelated" to the other decisions that 
comprise the series of decisions, the official is disqualified from talcing part in all of those 
decisions. (Mattas Advice Letter, No. A-16-113.) Regulation 18706(b) provides that "decisions are 
'inextricably interrelated' when the result of one decision will effectively determine, affinn, nullify, 
or alter the result of another decision." 
The decisions relating to various options for the at-grade crossings within the City are a

City, the decisions relating to the citywide options are inextricably interrelated to the other 
decisions regarding the at-grade crossings. Thus, at this time, it does not appear that the decisions 
relating to the City's at-grade crossings are capable of segmentation. 
I8702.5(c) provides that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.2 when the decision at issue affects the 
official's real property interest. 
3 Under Regulation 18703(b), a "significant segment of the public" is at least 25 percent of: all businesses or 
nonprofit entities within the jurisdiction (paragraph (l)); all real property, commercial real property, or residential real 
property within the jurisdiction (paragraph (2)); or all individuals within the jurisdiction (paragraph (3 )). You should 
seek further advice regarding the application of the public generally exception if the decisions at issue will affect a 
significant segment of the public.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit: 
(1) Mayor Kniss from taking part in governmental decisions relating to at-grade crossings of the 
Cal train rail corridor tracks that bisect the City given that Mayor Kniss has an ownership 
interest in a multi-family residential rental property located within 500 feet of the tracks? 
(2) Vice Mayor Filseth from taking part in those decisions given that he has an ownership interest 
in his single-family residence, located within 500 feet of the tracks and the Palo Alto Avenue at-
grade crossing? 
(3) Councilmember DuBois from taking part in those decisions given that he leases his residence 
located within 500 feet of the tracks and the Meadow Drive at-grade crossing?

CONCLUSION: (1) Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the at-grade crossings 
because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her 
business interest in her multi-family residential real property. 
(2) Yes. The Act prohibits the Vice Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would 
have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on his residence under Regulation 
18702.2(a)(l l). 
(3) No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because 
they would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his leasehold interest 
in his residence under Regulation l 8702.2(b ). 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR 
The Caltrain rail corridor bisects the City of Palo Alto, separating the northeastern portion of 
the City from the southwestern portion. The rail corridor runs parallel to Alma Street and passes 
close by many residential parcels within the City. The State's High Speed Rail Authority and 
Caltrain intend to increase both the frequency and length of trains running on these tracks. Toward 
that end, the Caltrain Joint Powers Board has begun a project to transition the Caltrain system from 
diesel to electric power. This project will change the infrastructure along the Caltrain system's 
right-of-way by adding aerial wires ("catenary wires"), thereby enabling the use of trains that are 
quieter, faster, and more frequent. Caltrain expects to begin electrified service in 2022. 
Responding in part to the prospect of more frequent trains, San Francisco Peninsula cities 
with east-west streets that cross the tracks at grade level, including the City of Palo Alto, have 
begun planning to separate those streets from the grade of the tracks to limit interruption of traffic 
circulation and to increase safety. The City currently has three separated roadway crossings and 
four at-grade roadway crossings of the tracks. 
The City has conducted vehicle circulation studies that have shown that increased gate-
down time at the at-grade crossings resulting from the increased use of the tracks will be felt 
throughout the City. However, it has not yet detennined which crossings will be modified, how they 
will be modified, or in what order they will be modified. Commencement of the City's grade-
crossing modification projects will not begin until 2023 at the earliest, and completion of the 
projects will take a decade or more. 
The City is currently engaged in preliminary planning for grade separating the four City 
streets that cross the tracks: Palo Alto A venue, Churchill A venue, Meadow Drive, and Charleston 
Road. There are numerous options for separating the train grade from that of cars, bicycles, and 
pedestrians at the City's at-grade crossings. A non-exhaustive list of potential options includes: 
raising the tracks above the street or depressing the street under the tracks; raising the street over the 
tracks; or closing each at-grade crossing. Depending on the configuration of the particular crossing 
at issue and the option or options selected, a grade separation could require public acquisition of 
nearby private property.  
In addition to considering projects at the four at-grade crossings, the planning process has 
also included consideration of"citywide options" in which the entire Caltrain right-of-way would 
be tnoved below ground level, into a trench or underground tunnel. Either of these citywide options 
would result in impacts along the entire length of the tracks, including necessary changes to the 
existing at-grade and grade-separated crossings and to the City's two existing train stations at 
University Avenue and California Avenue. 
All potential projects will involve at least some limited-term construction impacts. For some 
options, such as the citywide options, construction impacts would be substantial and last two to five 
years or longer. In addition, because trains must continue to run while projects are constructed, 
options that require the tracks to be moved will also require significant temporary replacement 
tracks to be built to enable ongoing operation of the corridor during construction. 
The Palo Alto City Council has nine members including the Mayor and Vice Mayor. Mayor 
Kniss has an ownership interest in a multi-family residential rental property in the City in which she 
does not reside. That property is within 500 feet of the Caltrain tracks, but is not nearby any of the 
City's four at-grade crossings. Vice Mayor Filseth has an ownership interest in his single-family 
residence, which serves as his primary residence, located within 500 feet of the tracks and the 
existing at-grade crossing at Palo Alto Avenue. 
Councilmember DuBois has a leasehold interest in his residence, a rented townhouse, 
located within 500 feet of the tracks and the at-grade crossing at Meadow Drive. Until recently, the 
Councilmember was a long-time home owner in the City. Three years ago, the Councilmember and 
his then-wife began marital dissolution proceedings and sold their home. The Councilmember 
subsequently rented a residence for a two-year period that ended on April 8, 2018, and he recently 
moved into the townhouse that serves as his current residence. The townhouse is convenient for his 
daughter, who has two years of high school remaining. 
You state that Councilmember DuBois intends to purchase a home soon, and is actively 
monitoring real estate listings and attending open houses. You also state that his lease can be broken 
at any time, and that if he breaks the lease, he would only be responsible for rent until the 
townhouse is re-rented. You also note that finding a new tenant for rental property within the City 
does not typically take long in the City's current rental market.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 May 30, 2018 Molly S. Stump Palo Alto City Attorney 250 Hamilton Avenue, 8th Floor Palo Alto, CA 94301 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-18-106 Dear Ms. Stump: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City of Palo Alto Mayor Liz Kniss, Vice Mayor Eric Filseth, and City Councilmember Tom DuBois regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTIONS Does the Act prohibit: (1) Mayor Kniss from taking part in governmental decisions relating to at-grade crossings of the Caltrain rail corridor tracks that bisect the City given that Mayor Kniss have an ownership interest in a multi-family residential rental property located within 500 feet of the tracks? (2) Vice Mayor Filseth from taking part in those decisions given that he has an ownership interest in his single-family residence, located within 500 feet of the tracks and the Palo Alto Avenue at-grade crossing? (3) Councilmember DuBois from taking part in those decisions given that he leases his residence located within 500 feet of the tracks and the Meadow Drive at-grade crossing? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-18-106 Page No. 2 CONCLUSIONS (1) Yes. The Act prohibits the Mayor from taking part in decisions relating to the at-grade crossings because those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her business interest in her multi-family residential real property. (2) Yes. The Act prohibits the Vice Mayor from taking part in those decisions because they would have a reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on his residence under Regulation 18702.2(a)(11). (3) No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on his leasehold interest in his residence under Regulation 18702.2(b). FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTOR The Caltrain rail corridor bisects the City of Palo Alto, separating the northeastern portion of the City from the southwestern portion.
